A method to capture practice genesis in real-time is essential for developing proactive theories of communication technology effects.
proposals.hypothesis: The initial user cohort of a novel communication system engages in a distinct, observable process of normative negotiation and feature adaptation that systematically shapes the platform's emergent social functions and content patterns, a process that can be modeled through longitudinal, multi-method data capture from platform launch.
proposals.method: We will develop a detection system using Python and the Twitter/Reddit APIs (pre-2026 standards) to identify nascent platforms via keyword monitoring and tech news aggregation. For selected tools, we will implement a mixed-method protocol: 1) computational trace data collection via instrumented browser extensions (with user consent) to log feature use sequences; 2) embedded ethnographic observation through scheduled participant interviews and screen-recording diaries with founding users; and 3) automated content analysis using LIWC and topic modeling (e.g., LDA) on early communication transcripts to track pattern stabilization.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a replicable methodological protocol for longitudinal platform studies, moving the field beyond snapshot or retrospective analysis. It will generate a dynamic model of practice genesis, contributing directly to theories of affordances, digital literacy development, and the early-stage formation of privacy norms and algorithmic biases in communication systems.
proposals.novelty: The proposal's novelty lies in its prospective, capture-from-launch design, which is rare in communication research, and its integration of computational log data with deep ethnographic immersion from a platform's earliest moments to trace micro-level adaptations that aggregate into macro-level norms.

proposals.title: Mapping the Digital Ecosystem: A Community Ecology Analysis of Functional Niches and Interaction Networks in Emerging Communication Platforms
proposals.motivation: The rapid emergence of novel computer-mediated communication platforms (e.g., social VR, AI-mediated communities) presents a challenge for understanding their internal dynamics and societal impact. Pre-2026 research often treats these platforms as monolithic or focuses on single features, lacking a holistic framework to explain how diverse user roles and content forms co-exist and interact to create a sustainable community. This gap limits our ability to predict platform evolution, assess risks to user privacy and digital literacy, and understand new forms of algorithmic bias.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying principles from community ecology, we hypothesize that a new digital communication platform can be modeled as a functional ecosystem where the stability and output of the community are determined by the diversity of user/content 'species', their occupation of distinct topical 'niches', and the structure of their 'trophic' interaction networks.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-method study of a nascent platform (e.g., a generative AI chat community). First, systematic content analysis and user surveys will catalog 'species' (user archetypes, bot types, content genres) and 'functional traits' (goals, expertise). Second, we will employ natural language processing (topic modeling with Latent Dirichlet Allocation) to define the platform's 'niche axes' (substantive topics). Third, we will construct and analyze interaction networks (using Gephi for visualization and metrics like centrality and modularity) to map 'trophic' relationships such as information provision, moderation, amplification, and support.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, integrative analytical framework for comparative platform studies, moving beyond feature lists to functional models. It will advance theory in digital media ecology by formally adapting ecological concepts, and offer practical insights for platform designers and policymakers concerned with community health, information flow, and equity.
proposals.novelty: While ecological metaphors are common, this study provides a rigorous, methodological translation of community ecology's core tools—systematic trait classification, niche modeling, and trophic network analysis—to the empirical study of a digital communication system, a novel synthesis for the field.

proposals.title: Automated Affordances and Digital Divides: A Content and Relational Analysis of AI Chatbots in Bureaucratic Communication
proposals.motivation: The rapid deployment of AI chatbots by government and educational institutions (pre-2026) promises efficiency but risks creating new forms of digital exclusion and opaque governance. Prior research on technology adoption and digital divides does not sufficiently analyze how the *content* and *relational functions* of these automated interactions reshape citizen-state dialogue. This study addresses a critical gap by examining how AI mediation constructs bureaucratic problems, solutions, and trust, directly impacting access, literacy, and privacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that AI-mediated bureaucratic communication will systematically differ from human-mediated exchanges in its linguistic patterns, topical focus, and relational affordances, privileging certain problem framings and user literacies while obscuring pathways for clarification and escalation, thereby reconfiguring institutional transparency and access.
proposals.method: We will collect and compare two corpora: 1) transcripts from AI chatbot interactions (e.g., municipal permit, university financial aid systems) and 2) analogous human-mediated exchanges (e.g., emails, call logs). Using a mixed-methods approach, we will conduct computational text analysis (topic modeling via Latent Dirichlet Allocation and Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count) to identify content patterns, supplemented by qualitative, affordance-based critical discourse analysis to code for relational functions like trust-building, clarification, and procedural guidance.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, integrative framework for analyzing the substantive and relational impacts of automated organizational communication, moving beyond adoption studies. It will provide empirical evidence on how AI systems transform bureaucratic dialogue, offering critical insights for policymakers on designing for transparency, equity, and effective service delivery in the digital public sphere.
proposals.novelty: While studies exist on AI adoption and algorithmic bias, this project uniquely fuses computational text analysis with a critical, affordance-based examination of the *relational functions* (e.g., trust, escalation) in AI-mediated bureaucracy, directly linking communication patterns to outcomes of access and institutional transparency.

proposals.title: Algorithmic Affordances and User Adaptation: A Content Analysis of Social Functions in Generative AI-Mediated Communication
proposals.motivation: The rapid adoption of generative AI systems like ChatGPT introduces novel computer-mediated communication (CMC) environments with distinct algorithmic affordances. Pre-2026, research has focused on user acceptance and bias, but there is a critical gap in understanding the substantive content and emergent social functions of interactions these systems catalyze. This matters for digital literacy and privacy, as these interactions reshape information seeking, social support, and creative collaboration online.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that user interactions with generative AI systems are characterized by distinct, system-prompted topical clusters and serve latent social-relational functions (e.g., companionship, coaching) that extend beyond their instrumental information-provision affordances.
proposals.method: We will collect a large corpus of publicly shared user-AI interaction transcripts from platforms like Reddit and dedicated AI forums. Using a mixed-methods approach, we will first apply unsupervised topic modeling (e.g., BERTopic or Latent Dirichlet Allocation) to identify dominant substantive themes. Second, we will conduct a qualitative, affordance-based content analysis to code for social functions, drawing on established CMC frameworks. Statistical analyses will examine relationships between topics, functions, and user demographics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a foundational map of the content ecology of generative AI-mediated communication, linking technical affordances to user adaptation. It will contribute to theories of media richness and the digital divide by showing how new literacies are enacted. The findings will offer evidence-based guidance for designing equitable AI systems and literacy interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines algorithmic bias or acceptability, this study uniquely analyzes the *content and social functions* of the interactions themselves, moving beyond the system or the user to focus on the emergent communication phenomena. It integrates Rice's work on ICT affordances with contemporary CMC analysis of a transformative technology.

proposals.title: Modeling the Digital Ecosystem of Care: An Ecological Network Analysis of Interaction Patterns, Clinical Outcomes, and Patient Privacy
proposals.motivation: Healthcare systems are increasingly digital, introducing new communication patterns (e.g., EHR alerts, patient portal messages) that fundamentally reshape clinical workflows. However, pre-2026 research often struggles to quantify the systemic impact of these digital affordances, treating outcomes like efficiency or errors in isolation without a framework to link interaction structure to performance. This gap is critical, as unmeasured structural changes can create digital divides in care quality and introduce new privacy vulnerabilities through unintended data flows.
proposals.hypothesis: The introduction of specific digital communication affordances (e.g., a new patient portal message type) will produce quantifiable, significant changes in the structural metrics (e.g., modularity, centrality) of a clinical interaction network, and these structural changes will be correlated with measurable variations in clinical efficiency (e.g., length of stay), error rates, and patient perceptions of privacy and satisfaction.
proposals.method: We will construct longitudinal, multi-modal interaction networks from observational data (EHR audit logs, communication records) in a hospital unit, where nodes are care team members, patients, and technologies, and edges are weighted by communication frequency and data type. Using pre-2026 ecological network analysis packages (e.g., `igraph`, `bipartite`), we will calculate time-series metrics like connectance, nestedness, and node centrality. A quasi-experimental, interrupted time-series design will be employed, comparing network structure and outcome metrics before and after the introduction of a targeted digital intervention. Statistical analyses (e.g., MRQAP, panel regression) will correlate structural shifts with outcome data.
proposals.contribution: This research provides a novel, quantitative framework for evaluating the systemic impact of health ICTs, moving beyond individual adoption studies. It contributes to communication theory by operationalizing the 'affordances' concept in network-structural terms. For practice, it offers a method to proactively model the consequences of digital tools on care coordination, efficiency, and patient privacy concerns.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct adaptation of quantitative ecosystem stability metrics (e.g., modularity) to human-technology communication systems in healthcare, creating a causal-mechanistic bridge between interaction patterns and multi-dimensional outcomes that existing organizational or diffusion theories do not provide.

proposals.title: Causal Affordances and Social Emergence: An Agent-Based and Experimental Test of Algorithmic Visibility on Network Structure and Information Privacy
proposals.motivation: The design of digital platforms—their software affordances—fundamentally shapes social interaction, network formation, and outcomes like privacy and information diffusion. However, pre-2026 research, including Rice's work on ICT affordances and algorithmic bias, often relies on descriptive case studies or surveys, which struggle to isolate causal mechanisms. A rigorous, causal methodology is needed to understand how specific, engineered features *generate* observed social system dynamics, moving from correlation to explanation.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, isolatable software affordances related to information visibility and control (e.g., asymmetric vs. symmetric visibility of connections) are generative mechanisms that cause distinct, predictable patterns in emergent social network structures and collective privacy behaviors.
proposals.method: We will first build generative, mechanistic models using NetLogo to create agent-based simulations. Agents will be programmed with simple behavioral rules (e.g., homophily, trust thresholds) and interact within environments where key affordances (reply structures, visibility algorithms) are systematically varied as independent variables. The simulation will output longitudinal network data and privacy outcomes (e.g., information sharing levels). Promising causal claims from the simulation will then be tested via a longitudinal, randomized experiment using a custom-built research prototype (a minimal social platform) deployed with a participant pool, allowing for controlled A/B testing of the identified affordances.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a formal, causal methodology for studying socio-technical systems, bridging communication science and computational social science. It will yield generalizable knowledge about how to engineer software for desired social outcomes, directly informing debates on digital literacy, privacy-by-design, and mitigating algorithmic bias.
proposals.novelty: It integrates generative, computational modeling (to formally hypothesize mechanisms) with rigorous experimental testing in a unified framework, moving beyond the descriptive or purely correlational approaches dominant in pre-2026 studies of technology affordances and social effects.

proposals.title: Mapping the Digital Backbone of Care: A Social Network Analysis of Clinician Communication Structures and Individual Patient Outcome Pathways
proposals.motivation: The rapid digitization of healthcare communication via EHRs and secure messaging creates complex, computer-mediated clinical networks whose structural properties are poorly understood. Pre-2026 research shows that communication failures are a leading cause of medical errors, but studies rarely link specific, time-stamped network dynamics to the care pathways of individual patients. This gap limits the ability to design proactive, network-based interventions for patient safety and care coordination.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific structural properties of digital clinical communication networks (e.g., high centralization, brokerage bottlenecks, weak tie clusters) are longitudinally associated with variances in time-sensitive clinical decisions and adverse individual patient outcomes, such as medication errors and preventable readmissions.
proposals.method: The study will employ longitudinal SNA on de-identified metadata from EHR-based communication systems (e.g., secure messages, consultation requests) within a multi-hospital system. We will compute dynamic network metrics (degree/betweenness centrality, tie strength, E-I index) for clinician networks surrounding specific patient cohorts. Using multi-level modeling and sequence analysis, these time-varying network measures will be linked to granular patient care pathway data (decision timestamps, medication administrations, lab results) and ultimate outcomes (error reports, readmissions within 30 days, patient satisfaction scores).
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a novel 'network-outcome pathway' model that specifies how information flow architectures concretely impact care delivery. It will advance health communication theory by integrating SNA with patient-level outcome measurement and provide evidence-based, actionable insights for healthcare administrators to redesign digital communication protocols and team structures to mitigate systemic risks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links general communication patterns to aggregate outcomes, this study's fusion of longitudinal SNA with fine-grained, individual patient care sequences is novel, moving from correlation to mechanistic modeling of how network bottlenecks propagate through specific clinical decisions.

proposals.title: Measuring Digital Privacy Literacy: A Dynamic Scale Development Framework Using Computational Theme Tracking and Survey Validation
proposals.motivation: The rapid evolution of digital platforms and algorithmic systems (e.g., social media, generative AI like ChatGPT) continuously reshapes public understanding and concerns around privacy, trust, and safety. Traditional, static survey scales struggle to capture these fluid, context-dependent constructs, leading to a measurement gap between academic research and lived user experience. This is critical for addressing digital divides and understanding how users perceive and navigate algorithmic bias, as highlighted in pre-2026 research.
proposals.hypothesis: A synergistic framework that computationally identifies emergent themes from large-scale online discourse and uses them to iteratively develop psychometric scales will produce more valid, timely, and ecologically grounded measures of complex digital literacy constructs (e.g., algorithmic privacy literacy) than static, a priori scale development alone.
proposals.method: We will first apply unsupervised NLP (BERTopic or Structural Topic Modeling) and supervised sentiment/concern classification to longitudinal datasets of relevant online communications (e.g., Reddit discussions on privacy, Twitter/X data on ChatGPT). Thematically clustered outputs will generate candidate scale items. These items will undergo iterative psychometric testing (exploratory/confirmatory factor analysis, reliability tests) via surveys deployed to U.S. college student and broader online panels. Validated scales will be redeployed, with new response data fed back into the computational theme tracking to monitor conceptual drift.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a novel, replicable methodology for dynamic scale development in communication and internet studies, directly addressing the challenge of measuring evolving constructs. It contributes substantive, validated scales for digital privacy literacy in the context of AI-mediated communication, bridging computational social science and traditional survey research.
proposals.novelty: The closed-loop integration of unsupervised computational theme discovery with rigorous psychometric validation creates a co-evolutionary measurement model, moving beyond using text analysis merely to validate existing scales or using static scales to categorize dynamic discourse.

proposals.title: Co-Constructing Context: A Participant-Activated Thick Description Method for Studying Privacy and Digital Literacy in Algorithmic Workarounds
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on digital literacy and privacy often relies on self-reports or lab studies, which lack ecological validity, or on passive sensing, which captures behavior but misses intent and social nuance. Understanding how users actively navigate and work around opaque algorithmic systems, such as social media feeds or search engines, requires rich, contextual data about in-situ technology use that minimizes observer effects. This gap limits our ability to design interventions that support user agency and literacy in complex digital environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A method combining passive, multi-modal sensing of technology use with structured participant review sessions will yield a more ecologically valid and interpretively rich account of user strategies—including workarounds and privacy behaviors—than either approach alone. This co-analytic process will reveal significant discrepancies between logged behavior and user-perceived intent, illuminating the cognitive and social dimensions of digital literacy.
proposals.method: We will recruit a diverse sample of participants to install a consented sensing suite on their primary devices (e.g., using pre-2026 tools like AWARE or custom scripts) to log application use, network traffic, and screen content (blurred for privacy). Concurrently, wearable sensors (e.g., Empatica E4) will capture physiological stress markers. After a two-week observation period, participants will engage in video-cued recall sessions using their own aggregated data visualizations, guided by a structured interview protocol to provide intent, emotional context, and social interpretations. Thematic analysis of these sessions, triangulated with sensor logs, will identify patterns of algorithmic workarounds and privacy management.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a novel methodological framework for communication and internet studies that bridges quantitative behavioral traces with qualitative user interpretation, advancing the study of technology affordances and digital divides. It will generate new insights into the cognitive and contextual factors driving user workarounds of algorithmic bias, directly extending Rice's prior work. The resulting thick descriptions will provide a richer evidence base for designing user-centric algorithms and literacy programs.
proposals.novelty: While sensor-aided ethnography and recall methods exist, this approach's novelty lies in systematically positioning the participant as a co-analyst of their own multi-modal behavioral data to generate thick description, specifically to investigate pre-2026 concerns about algorithmic workarounds and digital literacy. It moves beyond post-hoc interviews by grounding reflection in a comprehensive, temporally synchronized record of digital activity.

proposals.title: From Description to Prediction: A Computational Activity Theory Framework for Proactive Healthcare IT Governance
proposals.motivation: Healthcare systems face persistent challenges with technology adoption and workflow disruption, often analyzed post-hoc through qualitative frameworks like Activity Theory. However, these descriptive models lack predictive power. The increasing instrumentation of Electronic Health Records (EHRs) and communication platforms provides rich, real-time data on digital activity, creating an opportunity to move from retrospective analysis to proactive governance. This is critical for patient safety, clinician well-being, and equitable system performance, addressing core concerns of digital literacy and privacy in high-stakes environments.
proposals.hypothesis: That measurable digital traces (e.g., EHR interaction logs, secure message metadata, audit trails) can serve as valid proxies for core Activity Theory components (tools, rules, community, division of labor), and that contradictions within this instrumented activity system can be detected algorithmically to predict points of workflow breakdown before they critically impact care.
proposals.method: The research will develop a closed-loop framework integrating three computational layers. First, an instrumentation layer will map EHR API data, communication logs, and scheduling data to Activity Theory constructs (e.g., tool mediation via feature use frequency; rules via policy document access vs. workaround patterns). Second, a system dynamics layer will model relationships between these constructs, calibrated with historical data. Third, an anomaly detection layer (using Isolation Forest or autoencoder-based models) will continuously analyze the dynamic model's state for deviations signaling emerging contradictions. Simulations of interventions (e.g., a new module rollout) will be tested within this computational model.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel, operationalized methodological bridge between rich qualitative social theory and computational social science. It advances the study of organizational ICT affordances by making the concepts of contradiction and breakdown empirically measurable and predictable. For healthcare IT governance, it provides a evidence-based tool for proactive, human-centered system design, moving beyond post-implementation audits.
proposals.novelty: While Activity Theory is widely used descriptively, its translation into a predictive, computational framework for real-time system governance is novel. The integration of its sociological constructs with system dynamics and anomaly detection techniques to form a closed-loop advisory system represents a significant advance beyond existing diagnostic or evaluative applications.

proposals.title: Bridging Worlds, Bending Systems: A Comparative Analysis of Clinicians' Boundary-Spanning Practices with Health Information Technology
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of complex health information systems (HIS) like EHRs creates new digital divides and privacy challenges at the intersection of clinical practice, patient communication, and institutional demands. Clinicians must navigate these competing 'social worlds,' but research often examines single systems or settings, failing to separate individual workarounds from systematic affordances. A comparative, clinician-level analysis is crucial to understand how technology-in-use mediates these critical boundary-spanning activities that impact care quality, professional autonomy, and information equity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that clinicians' boundary-spanning practices (e.g., translating information, negotiating with teams, justifying decisions) are systematically shaped less by individual idiosyncrasy and more by the interaction between specific, perceived affordances of the HIS and the organizational context of their practice setting.
proposals.method: We will conduct a structured comparative case study of 24-30 clinicians across three distinct practice settings (e.g., academic hospital, community clinic, private practice). Data collection involves semi-structured interviews, artifact analysis, and observation, analyzed through a consistent framework of boundary-spanning activities. The analysis employs qualitative comparative analysis (QCA) and thematic coding using NVivo to trace how similar system features are variably enlisted, identifying patterns that distinguish individual adaptation from context-driven use.
proposals.contribution: This research contributes a robust, mid-range theory of system-mediated boundary spanning in healthcare, directly extending Rice's work on ICT affordances and organizational communication. It provides actionable insights for HIS design that supports, rather than hinders, clinicians' navigation of multiple social worlds, addressing digital literacy and privacy concerns at the point of care.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the structured comparative design that explicitly separates individual clinician agency from systematic technological/organizational influences, moving beyond single-case ethnographies to develop generalizable theory about how the same technology produces divergent communicative practices across contexts.

proposals.title: Mapping the Digital Habitat: A Landscape Ecology Approach to Clinician Workflow Integration and Digital Literacy
proposals.motivation: The fragmentation of clinical work across multiple digital systems (EHRs, communication platforms, knowledge tools) creates a complex 'digital landscape' that clinicians must navigate. This fragmentation can impede workflow, increase cognitive load, and create privacy risks, representing a critical digital literacy challenge in healthcare. Understanding how individual clinicians integrate these disparate systems is essential for designing tools that support, rather than hinder, professional practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a clinician's effective use of a central digital system (e.g., an EHR) functions as a boundary-spanning ecosystem, where the system's affordances for transferring context, knowledge, and social capital between professional 'habitat patches' predict the clinician's perceived workflow integration and digital literacy efficacy.
proposals.method: The study will employ a multi-method, within-subject design tracking a single clinician's digital activity over one week. Data collection will include automated screen and application logging (using pre-2026 tools like screen capture software and system event logs), a detailed digital activity diary, and a stimulated recall interview where the log data is reviewed with the participant. Analysis will involve creating a spatial-temporal map of the clinician's digital 'movement,' coding for boundary-spanning activities (e.g., information transfer, communication bridging), and identifying 'digital barriers' (friction points, workarounds) using qualitative thematic analysis grounded in the landscape ecology analogy.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, fine-grained model of individual-level system integration, moving beyond organizational adoption studies. It will advance theory by applying ecological concepts of habitat connectivity and fragmentation to digital literacy and workflow studies. Practically, it will generate a method for diagnosing specific digital barriers in clinical settings and offer design principles for more connective health information technologies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the conceptual framework and analytical lens of landscape ecology—studying an individual's movement and resource use in a fragmented habitat—to the problem of human-computer interaction and digital literacy in a professional context, a direct analogy not present in pre-2026 communication or health informatics literature.

proposals.title: From Interoperability to Trust: A Governance-First Framework for Viable Health Data Networks
proposals.motivation: Despite significant investment in technical interoperability (e.g., FHIR standards), the promise of seamless, inter-organizational health data exchange remains largely unfulfilled. Pre-2026 research consistently identifies non-technical barriers—including legal liability, competitive disincentives, and institutional mistrust—as the primary obstacles to collaborative care networks. This project addresses a critical gap by shifting focus from technical connectivity to the governance structures necessary for sustainable collaboration.
proposals.hypothesis: A governance-first design process, which co-creates legal and value-exchange frameworks with stakeholders prior to technical implementation, will lead to higher perceived trust, stronger institutional commitment, and more viable models for inter-organizational health data sharing than approaches that prioritize technical interoperability alone.
proposals.method: The research employs a multi-phase, participatory design methodology. First, we conduct a diagnostic analysis of disincentives via surveys and interviews with stakeholders (providers, payers, patients) in a target network. Second, we facilitate structured workshops to co-design prototype governance artifacts, such as data use agreements and value-distribution models. Third, we pilot these frameworks in a simulated environment using scenario-based testing and communication network analysis to evaluate their impact on perceived trust and collaborative intent.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a validated, process-oriented model for building sustainable health data networks, moving beyond technical specifications. It advances theory in organizational communication and technology affordances by demonstrating how governance structures constitute a primary affordance for collaboration. Practically, it provides a toolkit of co-designed legal and institutional templates for network stakeholders.
proposals.novelty: While most health IT research treats governance as a compliance issue following technical design, this project posits governance as the foundational, enabling architecture. It uniquely integrates Rice's expertise in organizational ICT affordances and user workarounds to solve adversarial collaboration problems in a high-stakes domain.

proposals.title: Protocols as Catalysts: An Ecological Analogy for Scaling Interoperability and Resilience in Healthcare Networks
proposals.motivation: Healthcare systems are fragmented, with separate organizations (hospitals, clinics, insurers) operating as autonomous entities, creating significant barriers to coordinated care, data sharing, and systemic resilience. Pre-2026, interoperability efforts often focus on monolithic data standards (like HL7 FHIR) or creating centralized data exchanges, which can be slow to scale and raise substantial privacy and competitive concerns. This research addresses the critical need for a lightweight, decentralized framework that can catalyze secure interactions without requiring deep organizational integration.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a neutral, protocol-based interoperability layer—inspired by ecological models of species interaction—will significantly improve network-level outcomes in healthcare systems, specifically increasing care coordination efficiency and enhancing the network's resilience to operational shocks (e.g., cyberattacks, pandemics) compared to ad-hoc or centralized exchange models.
proposals.method: We will design a prototype framework using a multi-agent system (MAS) architecture, where each healthcare organization is modeled as an autonomous agent. The core protocol will be a secure, event-driven digital handshake, implemented via a permissioned blockchain (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric) for auditability and smart contracts to encode interaction rules. Data flows will be abstracted using a neutral model akin to ecological interaction matrices, focusing on transaction types (e.g., 'referral', 'lab result query') rather than specific system identities. Network simulations will test the framework's impact on coordination and resilience metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel theoretical lens—ecological mutualism—to the study of organizational communication and information systems in healthcare. It provides a practical, scalable model for interoperability that balances organizational autonomy with systemic connectivity, directly addressing digital divides in access to coordinated care. The findings will offer evidence-based design principles for resilient, patient-centric health networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying ecological neutral theory and mutualistic interaction protocols to the socio-technical problem of healthcare interoperability, moving beyond traditional top-down standardization. This approach explicitly abstracts organizational identity to focus on rule-based engagement, a concept not prominent in pre-2026 health informatics or communication network literature.

proposals.title: Architectural Catalysis in Healthcare: A Communication Affordance Analysis of How Platform Design Enables and Governs Inter-Organizational Networks
proposals.motivation: The shift towards value-based and networked healthcare delivery (e.g., Accountable Care Organizations) critically depends on Health Information Exchanges (HIEs) and shared platforms. However, pre-2026 research often treats technology as a monolithic catalyst, neglecting how specific software architectures and features materially shape collaboration, trust, and power dynamics among autonomous organizations. This gap limits our ability to design systems that equitably govern data sharing and agency, directly impacting patient privacy, care coordination, and digital divides in health access.
proposals.hypothesis: The specific architectural design features of inter-organizational health platforms—particularly those governing data sovereignty, consent management, and audit transparency—directly constitute and regulate the communication affordances of awareness, visibility, and pervasiveness, which in turn structurally enable or constrain network-level collaboration, trust formation, and the allocation of agency among participating organizations.
proposals.method: The study employs a multi-method, comparative case study design. We will conduct in-depth interviews and communication network analysis with stakeholders (administrators, clinicians, IT staff) across 3-4 distinct HIE or ACO platforms selected for variance in key architectural features (e.g., centralized vs. federated data models, granularity of consent controls). Concurrently, we will perform a feature-level affordance analysis of the platforms' interfaces and governance protocols, mapping design choices to perceived and actualized affordances for action and interaction, as theorized in Rice's prior work on ICT affordances.
proposals.contribution: This research will generate a mid-range theory of 'architectural catalysis,' providing actionable design principles for inter-organizational systems that intentionally foster equitable and effective networks. It will extend affordance theory into the domain of multi-organizational governance and contribute empirical insights to debates on algorithmic bias, privacy, and digital literacy in complex institutional settings.
proposals.novelty: It moves beyond generic claims of technology as a catalyst to dissect the precise mechanisms—the material design features and their communicated affordances—through which software architecture actively governs inter-organizational relations, a synthesis not yet fully developed in pre-2026 communication or health informatics literature.

proposals.title: User-Driven System Evolution: A Multi-Method Study of Emergent Interactions and Platform Affordance Modification
proposals.motivation: Research on algorithmic systems and ICT affordances has predominantly focused on how systems shape user behavior, creating digital divides and privacy concerns. The inverse process—how collective user interactions, workarounds, and emergent literacy practices lead to tangible modifications of the system itself—remains understudied. Understanding this dynamic is crucial for a more complete model of socio-technical co-evolution, especially as generative AI tools like ChatGPT become embedded in educational and organizational contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that emergent user interactions, particularly those involving workarounds to perceived system biases or limitations, constitute a form of collective feedback that predictably influences subsequent technical and policy modifications by platform administrators.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal, multi-method case study of a major educational or collaborative platform (e.g., a Learning Management System or enterprise social media). Data collection will involve: 1) computational analysis of system change logs and version histories using process mining algorithms to identify modification events; 2) content and thematic analysis of user forum discussions, support tickets, and instructor/administrator communications to trace emergent interaction patterns and advocacy; and 3) interviews with both power users and system developers to map the perceived pathway from user practice to system change.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a grounded, process-oriented model of the 'inverse' socio-technical relationship, complementing Rice's prior work on ICT affordances and algorithmic bias. It will provide empirical evidence for how digital literacy and collective user agency operate as forces of system design, offering practical insights for more responsive and equitable platform governance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines user adaptation to systems, this proposal explicitly inverts the causal arrow to empirically trace how user practices become formalized as system features or policies, a non-obvious outcome of routine interaction.

proposals.title: Beyond Removal: A Configurational Model of ICT De-implementation and the Re-emergence of Face-to-Face Interaction in Organizations
proposals.motivation: Organizations increasingly face tensions between digital efficiency and human-centric work, with concerns that pervasive ICTs can erode social cohesion and create inequities. Pre-2026 research on the 'dark side' of ICTs and digital divides highlights the need to understand not just adoption but also intentional reduction or reconfiguration of systems. This study addresses a critical gap: under what specific conditions does removing or redesigning a system successfully catalyze desired face-to-face interaction, rather than simply creating disruption?
proposals.hypothesis: The successful re-emergence of face-to-face interaction following ICT de-implementation is not determined by removal alone, but by specific, conjunctural configurations of technical (e.g., system centrality), social (e.g., pre-existing communication norms), and policy (e.g., management support) preconditions.
proposals.method: We employ a comparative, longitudinal case study of 4-6 matched organizational pairs (e.g., university departments, corporate teams) where a similar system (e.g., a primary internal messaging platform) was either de-implemented or redesigned to be less interpositional. Data collection combines pre-/post-surveys measuring social cohesion, workflow efficiency, and perceived equity, with interviews and communication log analysis. We will use Qualitative Comparative Analysis (QCA), a set-theoretic method, to identify the necessary and sufficient conditions (configurations) leading to positive, neutral, or harmful outcomes for human interaction.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a mid-range contingency model that specifies the pathways to successful ICT de-implementation, moving the field from a simplistic 'removal is good/bad' debate to a nuanced, evidence-based framework. It will extend Rice's work on ICT affordances and the digital divide by applying a configurational lens to the understudied process of technology rollback and its social consequences.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines technology adoption and use, this study systematically investigates de-implementation as a deliberate organizational strategy, applying a configurational (QCA) approach to identify complex causal recipes rather than isolated net effects.

proposals.title: Engineered Digital Absence: A Controlled Experiment on the Catalytic Removal of Communication Technologies to Restore Complex Social Interaction
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on digital media and privacy highlights concerns that pervasive, always-on digital systems may act as simplifying scaffolds, potentially suppressing richer, face-to-face social ecosystems and creating new digital divides in communicative competence. This study addresses a critical gap by moving beyond measuring the effects of digital presence to actively testing the restorative social consequences of its planned removal, analogous to invasive species removal in ecological restoration.
proposals.hypothesis: The strategic, engineered removal of a specific, dominant digital communication system (the 'scaffold') from an organizational setting will catalyze a measurable cascade of compensatory, complex, analog social interactions, with the effect size moderated by pre-existing levels of digital literacy and the perceived pervasiveness of the removed technology.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal, mixed-methods field experiment in a university setting, using a crossover design. Student workgroups will be randomly assigned to conditions involving the scheduled removal of a dominant, group-specific digital tool (e.g., Slack, a course LMS) for one-week periods. Data collection will combine: 1) automated sensor-based tracking (via Sociometric badges pre-2026) of face-to-face interaction networks and vocal prosody; 2) pre/post surveys measuring perceived social affordances, privacy concerns, and digital literacy; and 3) ethnographic observation and diaries to capture emergent analog workarounds. Analysis will use multilevel modeling and thematic analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel, interventionist framework for studying technology's social role, shifting from a 'effects of presence' to a 'catalysis of absence' paradigm. It will generate empirical evidence on the conditions under which reducing digital mediation can restore complex social interaction, offering practical insights for digital well-being and organizational communication policy that account for digital divides.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct adaptation of the 'engineered absence' concept from restoration ecology to communication studies, and in its experimental focus on observing emergent social behaviors post-removal, rather than on deprivation or nostalgia, which are common in prior digital detox research.

proposals.title: Therapeutic De-implementation: A Communication-Centered Analysis of Digital Barrier Removal and Its Impact on Clinical Outcomes and Patient-Provider Relationships
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on health informatics overwhelmingly focuses on technology implementation, often documenting unintended negative consequences like clinician burnout, degraded communication, and patient dissatisfaction. This creates a critical gap: we lack systematic, evidence-based frameworks for deciding when and how to remove or redesign digital tools that harm care. Grounded in socio-technical systems theory, this study addresses the urgent need to optimize healthcare's digital ecosystem for human, not just technical, performance.
proposals.hypothesis: Strategically removing specific, well-documented digital workflow barriers (e.g., mandatory data-entry during patient intake, intrusive EHR alerts) will function as a positive intervention, leading to measurable improvements in clinical outcomes (e.g., reduced medication errors) and enhanced patient-provider communication as measured by validated relational metrics.
proposals.method: The study employs a multi-method, longitudinal field experiment in partnership with a hospital system. We will first conduct communication audits and affordance analyses to identify candidate digital barriers for removal. Using a stepped-wedge cluster randomized design, we will quantitatively track hard outcomes (error rates, length of stay) and administer pre/post surveys (using instruments like the Patient Satisfaction Questionnaire and Perceived Efficacy in Patient-Physician Interactions scale) alongside qualitative interviews and communication network analysis to map changes in interaction patterns following de-implementation.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a novel, evidence-based framework for 'therapeutic de-implementation,' shifting the paradigm from uncritical adoption to strategic optimization. It will contribute to the fields of health communication and informatics by rigorously linking specific digital affordances/constraints to both clinical and relational outcomes, offering actionable guidance for healthcare administrators.
proposals.novelty: It moves beyond critiquing technology to empirically testing removal as an intervention, and uniquely fuses communication theory (affordances, relational coordination) with clinical outcome measurement to validate the human-centric impact of simplifying digital ecosystems.

ideas.title: Comparative analysis of workarounds across domains
ideas.description: Investigate if and how user workarounds differ between contexts like healthcare, hiring, and social media.
ideas.sourceTitle: Understanding how users may work around algorithmic bias

ideas.title: Therapeutic De-implementation: Quantifying the Clinical and Relational Impact of Removing Digital Barriers in Healthcare
ideas.description: This research investigates the hypothesis that strategically removing specific computer systems or digital workflow barriers in clinical settings can function as a positive intervention to enhance patient care. It moves beyond analyzing new interaction patterns to rigorously link those patterns to hard clinical outcomes (e.g., medication error rates, length of stay, readmission rates) and validated patient satisfaction metrics. The synergy creates a new paradigm of 'therapeutic de-implementation,' where the decision to remove a technology is driven by empirical evidence of its negative impact on the human interactions essential for diagnosis, communication, and therapeutic alliance, ultimately aiming to optimize the socio-technical ecosystem for patient benefit.

ideas.title: Comparative Clinician-Level Analysis of System-Mediated Boundary Spanning Across Social Worlds
ideas.description: This research investigates how health information systems are used to bridge multiple social worlds by conducting a structured comparative case study of clinicians across different practice settings. It employs a consistent analytical framework to map common boundary-spanning activities (e.g., translating information for patients, negotiating with team members, justifying decisions to administrators) and traces how the same or similar systems are variably enlisted in these practices. By comparing data across multiple clinicians, the design separates individual idiosyncrasy from systematic affordances and constraints of the technology-in-use. This allows the development of a robust, mid-range theory about how system design and organizational context shape clinicians' work of navigating between competing professional, institutional, and patient-centered demands.

ideas.title: The Clinician as a Digital Silo-Buster: Investigating Individual Workarounds to Systemic Reinforcement
ideas.description: This research investigates the paradoxical role of clinical information systems by focusing on the individual clinician as the unit of analysis. It examines how a single clinician, who must operate across distinct professional, departmental, and patient-care worlds, employs deliberate workarounds, personal data artifacts, or unofficial communication channels via the very systems designed to standardize workflow. The core inquiry is whether these individual, bottom-up tactics to bridge personal silos are systematically thwarted by the system's architecture (reinforcing Idea A's inhibitor thesis) or if they can accumulate into effective, if unofficial, pathways for integration, thereby scaling the clinician's personal bridging effort into a de facto systemic feature.

ideas.title: Architectural Catalysis: How Inter-organizational System Design Enables and Governs Networked Healthcare
ideas.description: This research investigates how the specific architectural and feature-level design of shared software platforms (like HIEs, ACO platforms, or telehealth hubs) acts as a catalyst that structurally enables, constrains, and governs new forms of collaboration between autonomous healthcare organizations. It moves beyond studying computers as general catalysts to dissect how design choices—such as data sovereignty models, consent management features, and audit log transparency—materially influence trust, define roles, and allocate agency within the emerging network. The outcome is a theory of 'architectural catalysis' that provides actionable principles for designing inter-organizational systems that intentionally foster equitable, effective, and resilient network-level care delivery models.

ideas.title: Applying Participatory Action Research from Public Health to Digital Privacy
ideas.description: This research inverts the assumption of user mastery by directly adapting a solution from public health: Participatory Action Research (PAR). The analogous problem is engaging communities with low health literacy or deep distrust in medical systems, which is solved by making community members co-researchers in the entire process. Adapted to digital privacy, the study would recruit participants who feel mastered by technology as equal partners. Together, the academic and community co-researchers would define privacy concerns, prototype and test interventions (like simplified controls or alternative metaphors for data sharing), and assess their effectiveness, generating solutions that are inherently grounded in the users' reality and empowering by design.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Analysis of Software Affordances as Catalysts for Evolving Communication Norms in Healthcare
ideas.description: This research conducts a longitudinal, multi-method study within healthcare organizations to trace how specific, designed affordances in communication and collaboration software (e.g., EHR messaging systems, clinical coordination platforms) catalyze, accelerate, or constrain the evolution of new professional interaction patterns. It combines longitudinal interaction data with interviews and design analysis to model the causal pathways through which features initiate, stabilize, or disrupt communication norms, such as escalation protocols or inter-departmental consultation practices. The outcome is a framework linking micro-level design decisions to macro-level, temporal patterns of organizational communication, providing evidence-based guidance for intentionally designing systems that foster adaptive and resilient clinical workflows.

ideas.title: Prospective Longitudinal Capture of Communication Practice Genesis in Novel Mediated Systems
ideas.description: This research establishes a protocol for the real-time study of communication practice formation from platform launch. It involves creating a detection system for new or radically updated communication tools, instrumenting them for data collection from their earliest user cohorts, and deploying embedded ethnographic and computational observation. The approach captures the initial negotiation of norms, the adaptation of features to unexpected uses, and the stabilization of early substantive patterns, providing a dynamic model of how communication content and social function co-evolve with technological affordances.

ideas.title: Governance-first frameworks for viable inter-organizational health data networks
ideas.description: This research directly confronts the non-technical barriers that stifle inter-organizational collaboration in healthcare. It begins by systematically diagnosing the specific legal, competitive, and institutional disincentives to sharing data and coordinating care within a target network. Using participatory design with stakeholders from multiple organizations, the project then develops and pilots prototype governance and legal frameworks—such as data use agreements, value-exchange models, and accountability mechanisms—that are enabled by, rather than driven by, interoperable computing systems. The outcome is a validated model for a sustainable collaborative network where technology serves a governance structure designed to build trust and align incentives.

ideas.title: Disentangling Structural Drivers: A Multi-Attribute Analysis of Organizational Mediators on Healthcare Technology Adoption and Impact
ideas.description: This study moves beyond comparing nominal healthcare organization types to isolate the specific structural attributes that explain differences in the adoption and efficacy of digital tools. It develops a framework measuring key organizational dimensions—such as governance centralization, reimbursement model flexibility, and interoperability capacity—across a diverse sample of provider entities. The research then analyzes how these measurable attributes mediate the relationship between technology interventions and outcomes like cost, quality, and access. This approach reveals the actionable organizational mechanics that determine whether a technology acts as a catalyst, rather than relying on heterogeneous, catch-all categories.

ideas.title: A Protocol-Based Interoperability Framework for Healthcare Networks
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from ecology to the problem of scaling computer-catalyzed interactions between separate healthcare organizations. In ecology, the complex, mutualistic interactions between diverse species (like plants and pollinators) are studied using neutral models and standardized interaction protocols that abstract away specific identities to focus on the rules of engagement. The adapted idea is to develop an analogous, computer-mediated protocol framework for healthcare networks. This framework would define standardized, secure digital 'handshakes' for data, patient, and resource flows between autonomous organizations, acting as a catalyst. By implementing this protocol-based layer, researchers can examine how such rule-based interactions affect network-wide properties like care coordination efficiency, systemic resilience to shocks, and the diffusion of best practices across the organizational ecosystem.

ideas.title: The Clinician as a Boundary-Spanning Ecosystem: Applying Landscape Ecology to Individual Workflow Integration
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy from landscape ecology. The analogous problem is understanding how a single organism survives and functions within a fragmented ecosystem. Ecologists solve this by intensively tracking the individual's movement between habitat patches, mapping its resource use, and analyzing the connective structures it relies on. Adapted to the clinical context, the study would investigate a single clinician's use of a digital system (e.g., an EHR, communication platform, or personal knowledge tool) to navigate their fragmented professional 'landscape'. Using methods like detailed activity logs, screen recording, and stimulated recall interviews, the research would map the clinician's digital pathways to identify how the system bridges their multiple social worlds—transferring context, knowledge, and social capital—and where disconnects or 'digital barriers' exist. The outcome is a fine-grained model of individual-level system integration as a boundary-spanning ecological process.

ideas.title: Modeling Habituated and Resigned Privacy as an Embedded Contradiction in Collective Healthcare Activity Systems
ideas.description: This research applies the activity theory framework to move beyond studying individual privacy management and instead model how automatic, habitual, or resigned privacy behaviors are systematically generated within collective healthcare activities. It posits that the computer system (tool) mediates not only care coordination but also privacy erosion, creating contradictions between the object of patient care and the rules/community of the healthcare institution. The analysis would examine how these systemic contradictions become resolved through patient and provider habituation or resignation, thereby stabilizing a system where privacy loss is an unexamined, embedded byproduct of routine healthcare activity.

ideas.title: The Contingent Architecture of Digital Silos: Mapping System Design to Organizational Boundary Dynamics
ideas.description: This research program investigates how specific architectural and governance features of computer systems shape the flow of information across organizational boundaries. Instead of asking if systems are inhibitors or catalysts, it maps the contingent mechanisms—such as access controls, data schemas, interoperability protocols, and algorithmic filtering—that simultaneously enable connection for some groups while reinforcing separation for others. Using a mixed-method design of computational ethnography, social network analysis, and comparative case studies, it tests which configurations of technical and social factors predict bridging versus siloing outcomes. The goal is to produce a diagnostic framework for designing systems that actively manage, rather than inadvertently harden, organizational boundaries.

ideas.title: Longitudinal study of computer-mediated interaction changes using ecological succession modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the framework of ecological succession from ecology to study how new computer-facilitated communication patterns evolve and stabilize over time within healthcare organizations. The analogous solution involves modeling the communication ecosystem, where new technologies are introduced as pioneer species. The adapted longitudinal study will track the sequence of adoption, the displacement or integration of old methods, the formation of new collaborative norms, and the eventual reach of a stable state, identifying the internal and external factors that drive or disrupt this succession, much as ecologists model the development of a mature forest from a barren plot.

ideas.title: Co-Investigating Oppressive Design: A Participatory Audit of Technology That Masters Its Users
ideas.description: This research shifts the focus from user deficits to systemic design failures by partnering with communities who feel mastered by technology. Using participatory action research methods, community co-investigators and academic researchers collaboratively audit specific digital platforms and services to identify dark patterns, coercive flows, and exploitative data practices. The outcome is a publicly shared, actionable audit framework and a set of design principles for 'humble technology' that respects user autonomy, moving beyond mere study to empower community-led advocacy and redesign.

ideas.title: Scale up to societal-level effects
ideas.description: Model the aggregate impact of widespread user workarounds on algorithmic system performance and equity.
ideas.sourceTitle: Understanding how users may work around algorithmic bias

ideas.title: SES gaps diminish with new technology adoption
ideas.description: Invert the assumption that AI access equalizes; SES may shape AI literacy more.
ideas.sourceTitle: College students’ literacy, ChatGPT activities, educational outcomes, and trust from a digital divide perspective

ideas.title: Operationalizing Activity Theory: A Closed-Loop Framework for Predictive Healthcare System Governance
ideas.description: This research develops a computational framework that translates the descriptive models of activity theory into a dynamic, instrumented system for healthcare. It defines measurable proxies for activity system components (tools, rules, community) and their relationships, enabling continuous monitoring for signs of contradiction and impending breakdown. Using techniques from system dynamics and anomaly detection, the framework can predict friction points, such as a new EHR module disrupting team coordination, and simulate the effects of potential interventions. This creates a closed-loop system for evidence-based, proactive healthcare IT governance that moves beyond description to prediction and prescriptive guidance.

ideas.title: The Contextual Valence of Mediated Interaction: A Framework for Predicting Outcomes of Computer-Catalyzed Social Exchange
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond a simplistic good/bad dichotomy to develop a predictive framework for the outcomes of technology-mediated interactions. It systematically identifies key moderating variables—such as platform architecture, temporal patterns, relational context, and individual differences—that determine whether an interaction catalyses conflict, overload, productive collaboration, or social bonding. The approach integrates data from social media platforms, communication tools, and virtual environments to build causal models of interaction valence. The ultimate goal is to generate evidence-based design principles and policies that can steer the development of future systems toward more consistently beneficial outcomes.

ideas.title: Network-Outcome Mapping: Linking SNA Metrics of Digital Health Networks to Patient Care Pathways
ideas.description: This research fuses social network analysis (SNA) with patient outcome measurement to investigate how the evolving structure of computer-mediated clinical communication networks (e.g., via EHR messaging, telehealth platforms, or secure chat) directly influences the trajectory and quality of patient care. It goes beyond linking general interaction patterns to aggregate metrics by using longitudinal SNA (centrality, tie strength, brokerage) to model how information flow and coordination bottlenecks at the clinician-network level propagate to affect specific, time-sensitive clinical decisions and, ultimately, individual patient outcomes like medication errors, readmission rates, or satisfaction. The novel synergy is a 'network-outcome pathway' model that can identify high-leverage network interventions—such as strategically altering communication routing or team composition—to proactively improve care efficiency and safety.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Psychosocial Costs of Computer-Catalyzed Interactions
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a quantitative survey instrument designed to measure the latent negative constructs—such as conflict escalation, trust degradation, safety concerns, and cognitive overload—that emerge from computer-catalyzed social interactions. By grounding scale development in qualitative themes from prior cluster analysis, the instrument moves beyond testing whether interactions are beneficial to diagnosing precisely how and why they become harmful. This enables researchers to model the relationships between interaction features (e.g., platform, anonymity, pace) and specific psychosocial costs, providing a predictive framework for assessing the risks of new connective technologies before widespread adoption.

ideas.title: Social network analysis of technology diffusion
ideas.description: Map communication ties to understand how word processing spread within organizations.
ideas.sourceTitle: Managing Organizational Innovation: The Evolution from Word Processing to Office Information Systems

ideas.title: Leverage social network analysis
ideas.description: Map the diffusion of workaround strategies through social networks to understand collective adaptation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Understanding how users may work around algorithmic bias

ideas.title: Inverse problem: interactions shaping systems
ideas.description: Study how emergent social interactions lead to modifications of the computer system itself.
ideas.sourceTitle: Bringing social worlds together: computers as catalysts for new interactions in health care organizations.

ideas.title: Analyze content of new computer-mediated communications
ideas.description: Study the substantive topics and social functions of interactions catalyzed by new systems.
ideas.sourceTitle: Bringing social worlds together: computers as catalysts for new interactions in health care organizations.

ideas.title: Security and compliance analysis for health databases
ideas.description: Analyze how each database technology supports healthcare-specific security, privacy, and regulatory compliance requirements.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Comparative Features Analysis of Publicly Accessible Commercial and Government Health Database Web Sites

ideas.title: Analyzing the Content and Function of AI-Mediated Citizen-Bureaucracy Communications
ideas.description: This research investigates the content and social functions of interactions within government agencies and large educational institutions that are mediated by newly deployed AI communication systems (e.g., chatbots for permit applications, virtual advisors for student services). It moves beyond testing adoption to critically analyze the substantive topics, linguistic patterns, and relational functions (e.g., trust-building, clarification, escalation) that these tools catalyze in bureaucratic settings. The goal is to understand how the implementation of such systems transforms the nature of citizen-state or student-institution dialogue, potentially altering access, transparency, and the very construction of bureaucratic problems and solutions through automated communication.

ideas.title: Causal Modeling and Experimental Testing of Software Affordances in Social System Emergence
ideas.description: This research program establishes a causal methodology for studying how software design shapes social interaction. It begins by building generative, mechanistic models (e.g., agent-based simulations) to formally hypothesize how specific, isolatable platform features (like reply structures or visibility algorithms) cause distinct patterns of network formation and group dynamics. These computationally derived causal claims are then rigorously tested through longitudinal, randomized experiments, either via collaboration with platforms for in-situ A/B testing or through deployment of custom research prototypes. The integrated approach moves beyond descriptive case studies to produce generalizable, causal knowledge about the engineering of social systems.

ideas.title: Challenge human-environment interaction as primary driver
ideas.description: Investigate if algorithmic curation, not physical environment, is the primary shaper of digital exposure and access.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hidden Gem: Archea (1977)

ideas.title: Participant-Activated Thick Description of Embedded Tech Use
ideas.description: This approach captures technology-in-context data without a researcher's immediate presence by deploying a suite of passive, consented sensors in the environment. Following the period of observation, participants engage in structured review sessions of their own data, acting as co-analysts to provide meaning, intent, and social nuance. This generates an ethnographic account that is grounded in direct experience yet avoids the behavioral distortions caused by an observer. The result is a more ecologically valid and interpretively rich understanding of catalyzed interactions.

ideas.title: Dynamic Scale Development via Computational Theme Tracking and Psychometric Validation
ideas.description: This research program integrates computational text mining of catalyzed communications with quantitative scale development to create a dynamic, data-driven framework for measuring complex social constructs. First, unsupervised and supervised NLP techniques (e.g., topic modeling, sentiment analysis) are applied to large-scale message data to identify, cluster, and track the emergence and evolution of key qualitative themes like safety and trust over time. These computationally derived themes then directly inform the iterative creation and rigorous psychometric testing of survey scales, whose items are grounded in the actual language and concerns of the population. The resulting validated scales can be redeployed to collect new data, which in turn feeds back into the text analysis, creating a synergistic loop that allows constructs and their measurements to co-evolve with real-world discourse.

ideas.title: Quantifying Interaction Pattern Impact via Ecological Network Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from ecosystem ecology to measure the impact of new interaction patterns on patient care outcomes. In ecology, network analysis is used to quantify how species interaction patterns (like predator-prey links) affect ecosystem-level outcomes such as stability and productivity. The analogous solution involves constructing interaction networks from observational data, calculating structural metrics (e.g., connectance, modularity), and correlating them with system performance. Adapted to healthcare, the idea is to model the clinical environment as an interaction network where nodes are care team members, patients, and technologies, and edges are their communications or data exchanges. By applying ecological network metrics to this map, researchers can quantitatively link the introduction of new digital interaction patterns (e.g., a new alert pathway or a patient portal message type) to changes in network structure. These structural changes can then be statistically correlated with hard clinical efficiency metrics (like medication error rates or length of stay) and patient satisfaction scores, providing a causal-mechanistic framework to measure the specific impact of technological interventions on care outcomes.

ideas.title: Configurational Analysis: When and How De-implementing Systems Reshapes Interaction
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a simple 'removal' thesis to investigate the conditions under which de-implementing or reconfiguring a computer system successfully catalyzes desired face-to-face interactions. It employs a comparative, longitudinal case study method, analyzing matched organizational pairs where a system was either removed or intentionally redesigned to be less interpositional. The study measures a spectrum of outcomes, including social cohesion, workflow efficiency, and equity, to build a contingency model. This model identifies the specific technical, social, and policy preconditions that predict whether removal will be beneficial, neutral, or harmful to human interaction.

ideas.title: Incorporate game theory for strategic interactions
ideas.description: Model the intervention as a game between the planner and network members with different incentives.
ideas.sourceTitle: Who Knows Your HIV Status II?: Information Propagation Within Social Networks of Seropositive People

ideas.title: Testing for 'Digital Allopatry': Do new computer systems create technological barriers that isolate organizational groups?
ideas.description: This idea adapts the biogeographical concept of allopatric speciation to organizational technology studies. In biology, a new physical barrier can split a population, leading to isolation and divergence. Analogously, a new computer system might act as a 'digital barrier,' isolating departments or teams by creating incompatible data formats, access permissions, or workflow logic that reinforce existing silos instead of bridging them. The analogous solution involves measuring vicariance and divergence. Adapted, the research would quantitatively test if a new system implementation causes a 'digital allopatry' event. This would be done by developing metrics for reduced cross-silo information flow, collaboration, and procedural alignment post-implementation, treating the technology as the isolating variable and measuring the divergence in practices and knowledge between the now-separated groups.

ideas.title: Technology as primary driver of change
ideas.description: Investigate cases where social factors completely reshape the intended use of technology.
ideas.sourceTitle: Managing Organizational Innovation: The Evolution from Word Processing to Office Information Systems

ideas.title: Longitudinal Social Network Analysis of Privacy Diffusion and Evolution
ideas.description: This research conducts a longitudinal study that tracks participants' privacy perceptions and behaviors across their devices while simultaneously mapping and analyzing the evolution of their social networks (both online and offline). By applying SNA metrics repeatedly over time, the study can quantify how changes in an individual's network position (e.g., increased centrality, new brokerage roles) correlate with shifts in their privacy attitudes and actions. This approach reveals the social contagion and structural drivers of privacy norms, moving beyond static snapshots to model the co-evolution of personal networks and privacy decision-making.

ideas.title: Life-Stage Contingent System Co-Construction
ideas.description: This research program investigates how the emergent social interactions that modify a computer system are themselves contingent on the life stage and demographic composition of the user base. It posits that systems co-constructed by, for example, older adults, teenagers, or working professionals will evolve along divergent functional and normative pathways compared to those shaped primarily by college students. The core synergy is a reciprocal model: life stage influences interaction patterns, which then sculpt the system, which in turn differentially impacts communities at different life stages, challenging assumptions of a uniform 'user-driven' evolution.

ideas.title: Analyzing the 'Ecological Niches' and 'Trophic Interactions' of New Digital Communication Platforms
ideas.description: This research adapts methodologies from community ecology to study the substantive topics and social functions within new computer-mediated communication systems. The analogous problem in ecology involves characterizing a new or disturbed ecosystem by identifying the functional niches occupied by different species and mapping the trophic interactions (e.g., predation, symbiosis) that define the community's structure. The analogous solution employs systematic surveys, network analysis, and functional trait classification to model energy flow and community stability. Adapted to the research context, this involves treating a new communication platform (e.g., a novel social VR space, a generative AI chat community) as a digital ecosystem. Researchers would catalog the 'species' (user archetypes, bot types, content forms), define the 'niche axes' (substantive topics like support, entertainment, coordination), and map the 'trophic interactions' (social functions like moderating, amplifying, gatekeeping, or providing emotional labor). The outcome is a functional model of the platform's communication ecology, revealing how its unique structure catalyzes specific types of interactions and sustains its community.

ideas.title: Removing the Digital Scaffold: Catalyzing Social Reconnection Through Engineered Absence
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from Ecosystem Restoration Ecology to the original seed idea. In restoration, practitioners use 'engineered absence'—the planned removal of a dominant, simplifying element like an invasive species or dam—to catalyze the recovery of complex, native ecological interactions. Analogously, this study proposes treating pervasive computer systems as a form of 'digital scaffold' that can simplify and suppress rich, face-to-face social ecosystems. The adapted research idea is to conduct controlled experiments where specific digital systems are strategically removed (engineered digital absence) from settings like workplaces or classrooms. The focus is not on the loss of digital function, but on actively observing and measuring the cascade of new, compensatory analog interactions—impromptu meetings, note-passing, re-purposed physical spaces—that emerge, thereby mapping the conditions under which removing a computer barrier most effectively catalyzes human reconnection and complex social restoration.

ideas.title: Use technology acceptance model for AI tool adoption
ideas.description: Apply the Technology Acceptance Model to analyze perceived usefulness and ease of use of ChatGPT.
ideas.sourceTitle: U.S. college students' acceptability and educational benefits of ChatGPT from a digital divide perspective

ideas.title: Scale up to massive online social networks
ideas.description: Apply the deviation-tolerant POMDP planner to influence campaigns on platforms like Facebook.
ideas.sourceTitle: Who Knows Your HIV Status II?: Information Propagation Within Social Networks of Seropositive People

ideas.title: The Affective Privacy Paradox in Integrated Ecosystems
